overview.summary: A sophisticated cyber attack orchestrated by the Unitronics Defacement Campaign targets transportation systems with a multi-stage approach. The attackers engage in extensive reconnaissance to map out the network topology, acquire necessary infrastructure for command and control, and exploit vulnerabilities for initial access. They maintain persistence, escalate privileges, evade defenses, access credentials, discover further systems, move laterally, collect sensitive data, exfiltrate information, and finally impact operations through defacement.

Scenario.attack_surface: AVAILABLE_SURFACES: Internet-facing assets, Command & Control Server, Network Traffic, Public IP/Network Block, File, Logon Session, Target System Artifact
Scenario.attack_vector: AVAILABLE_VECTORS: General Access Method, Network Traffic Flow (Mechanism), Response Metadata (Mechanism), Application Log Content (Mechanism), File Modification (Mechanism), Network Service Scanning, Network Traffic Flow (Mechanism), Logon Session Creation (Mechanism), Exfiltration Over C2 Channel, Network Traffic Content (Mechanism)

stage.name: Reconnaissance
stage.description: The attackers conduct active scanning to map the network topology of the transportation systems, identifying potential entry points and vulnerabilities.

stage.name: Resource Development
stage.description: The attackers establish command and control servers and other resources needed to support the operation, ensuring communication channels remain covert.

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.description: By compromising a supplier or partner, the attackers gain initial access to the transportation system's network.

stage.name: Execution
stage.description: The attackers exploit known vulnerabilities in public-facing transportation management systems to execute malicious payloads.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.capec: CAPEC-650: Upload a Web Shell to a Web Server
stage.description: To maintain access, the attackers deploy a web shell on the compromised server, providing a backdoor for continuous interaction.

stage.name: Privilege Escalation
stage.description: The attackers exploit weaknesses to elevate their privileges, gaining administrative control over critical system components.

stage.name: Defense Evasion
stage.description: To avoid detection, the attackers alter system images and logs to erase evidence of their presence and actions.

stage.name: Credential Access
stage.capec: CAPEC-226: Session Credential Falsification through Manipulation
stage.description: The attackers steal credentials to facilitate lateral movement within the network using tools like Mimikatz.

stage.name: Discovery
stage.description: After obtaining credentials, the attackers scan the network to identify additional systems and services for further exploitation.

stage.name: Lateral Movement
stage.description: The attackers leverage remote services like RDP or SSH to move laterally across the network, expanding their reach.

stage.name: Collection
stage.description: The attackers gather sensitive data from repositories, focusing on operational procedures and schedules.

stage.name: Exfiltration
stage.capec: CAPEC-24: Filter Failure through Buffer Overflow
stage.description: Stolen data is transferred to attacker-controlled servers using established command and control channels.

stage.name: Impact
stage.capec: CAPEC-675: Retrieve Data from Decommissioned Devices
stage.description: The attackers disrupt operations by altering visual content, such as changing signage or dashboard displays, consistent with the 'Unitronics Defacement Campaign'.

Scenario.summary: The Unitronics Defacement Campaign employs a complex, multi-stage cyber attack strategy targeting transportation systems. With expert skills, they meticulously plan and execute each phase, from reconnaissance to impact, leaving no stone unturned in their quest for disruption and data theft.
